perturbation.original_text: 1.54 "Losses" means any and all damages (including all incidental, consequential, statutory an treble damages), awards, deficiencies, settlement amounts, defaults, assessments, fines, dues, penalties, costs, fees, liabilities, obligations, taxes, liens, losses, lost profits and expenses (including without limitation court costs, interest and reasonable fees of attorneys, accountants and other experts) incurred by or awarded to Third Parties and required to be paid to Third Parties with respect to a Claim by reason of any judgment, order, decree, stipulation or injunction, or any settlement entered into in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement, together with all documented out-of-pocket costs and expenses incurred in complying with any judgments, orders, decrees, stipulations and injunctions that arise from or relate to a Claim of a Third Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.54 "Losses" means any and all damages (including all incidental damages), awards, deficiencies, settlement amounts, defaults, assessments, fines, dues, penalties, costs, fees, liabilities, obligations, taxes, liens, losses, lost profits and expenses (including without limitation court costs, interest and reasonable fees of attorneys, accountants and other experts) incurred by or awarded to Third Parties and required to be paid to Third Parties with respect to a Claim by reason of any judgment, order, decree, stipulation or injunction, or any settlement entered into in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement, together with all documented out-of-pocket costs and expenses incurred in complying with any judgments, orders, decrees, stipulations and injunctions that arise from or relate to a Claim of a Third Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "consequential, statutory and treble damages", the definition of Losses is significantly narrowed, conflicting with standard legal interpretations of recoverable damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-715 explicitly defines consequential damages in contract law, and this modification attempts to remove that coverage. This could result in disputes over the extent of liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-715. Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-715. Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages. (1) Incidental damages resulting from the seller's breach include expenses reasonably incurred in inspection, receipt , transportation and care and custody of goods rightfully rejected, any commercially reasonable charges, expenses or commissions in connection with effecting cover and any other reasonable expense incident to the delay or other breach. (2) Consequential damages resulting from the seller's breach include (a) any loss resulting from general or particular requirements and needs of which the seller at the time of contracting had reason to know and which could not reasonably be prevented by cover or otherwise; and (b) injury to person or property proximately resulting from any breach of warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract text included "consequential, statutory and treble damages" in the definition of 'Losses'. The changed text removes "consequential, statutory and treble damages", narrowing the definition. The Law Explanation states that UCC § 2-715 explicitly defines consequential damages in contract law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines consequential damages as 'any loss resulting from general or particular requirements and needs of which the seller at the time of contracting had reason to know and which could not reasonably be prevented by cover or otherwise'. By removing 'consequential damages', the changed contract contradicts UCC § 2-715 and potentially limits the buyer's ability to recover foreseeable losses, creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.17 "Confidential Information" means all secret, confidential or proprietary information, data or Know-How (including GSK Know-How and Theravance Know-How) whether provided in written, oral, graphic, video, computer or other form, provided by one Party (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party (the "Receiving Party") pursuant to this Agreement or generated pursuant to this Agreement, including but not limited to, information relating to the Disclosing Party's existing or proposed research, development efforts, patent applications, business or products, the terms of this Agreement and any other materials that have not been made available by the Disclosing Party to the general public. Confidential Information shall not include any information or materials that the Receiving Party can document with competent written proof:
perturbation.changed_text: 1.17 "Confidential Information" means all secret, confidential or proprietary information, data or Know-How (including GSK Know-How and Theravance Know-How) whether provided in written, oral, graphic, video, computer or other form, provided by one Party (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party (the "Receiving Party") pursuant to this Agreement or generated pursuant to this Agreement, including but not limited to, information relating to the Disclosing Party's existing or proposed research, development efforts, patent applications, business or products, the terms of this Agreement and any other materials that have not been made available by the Disclosing Party to the general public. Confidential Information shall not include any information or materials that the Receiving Party believes is not confidential.
perturbation.explanation: This modification makes the determination of what constitutes 'Confidential Information' subjective and based on the Receiving Party's belief, which contradicts standard trade secret and confidentiality protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act defines trade secrets objectively based on reasonable measures to keep information secret, not on subjective beliefs. This change severely weakens confidentiality protections.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a subjective standard ('Receiving Party believes is not confidential') for determining Confidential Information, which directly contradicts the objective standard required for trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that the Defend Trade Secrets Act defines trade secrets objectively based on reasonable measures to keep information secret. The modified text's reliance on subjective belief weakens the confidentiality protection, rendering it difficult to enforce trade secret protection under the DTSA. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 are not helpful, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both point to how the changes are at odds with established law.

perturbation.original_text: 1.31 "First Commercial Sale" means the first shipment of commercial quantities of any Collaboration Product sold to a Third Party by a Party or its sublicensees in any Country after receipt of Marketing Authorization Approval for such Collaboration Product in such Country. Sales for test marketing, sampling and promotional uses, clinical trial purposes or compassionate or similar uses shall not be considered to constitute a First Commercial Sale.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.31 "First Commercial Sale" means the first shipment of commercial quantities of any Collaboration Product sold to a Third Party by a Party or its sublicensees in any Country, regardless of whether Marketing Authorization Approval for such Collaboration Product in such Country has been obtained. Sales for test marketing, sampling and promotional uses, clinical trial purposes or compassionate or similar uses shall not be considered to constitute a First Commercial Sale.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for 'Marketing Authorization Approval,' the definition of First Commercial Sale now contradicts regulations requiring approval before commercial sale of pharmaceutical products.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, 21 U.S.C. § 505
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. § 505 requires FDA approval before a new drug can be commercially sold. This modification would permit sales prior to approval, in violation of federal law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Laws Enforced by FDA Home Regulatory Information Laws Enforced by FDA Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) Laws Enforced by FDA FD&C Act Reference Information United States Code, Title 21 The laws of the United States are organized by subject into the United States Code. The United States Code contains only the currently enacted statutory language. The official United States Code is maintained by the Office of the Law Revision Counsel in the United States House of Representatives. The Office of the Law Revision Counsel reviews enacted laws and determines where the statutory language should be codified related to its topic. The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act and subsequent amending statutes are codified into Title 21 Chapter 9 of the United States Code. The listing of FD&C Act sections presented here identifies both the FD&C Act and U.S. Code section numbers, which can be used to narrow your search on the Law Revision Counsel website. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, use the Advanced Search to limit the your search to Title 21. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, you may either search by U.S. code section number or browse the Title 21 section listing . Content current as of: 03/29/2018 Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for 'Marketing Authorization Approval' before a 'First Commercial Sale,' directly contradicting the necessity of FDA approval before commercial sale as per 21 U.S.C. § 505. The scraped snippet from the FDA website references the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) which is codified into Title 21 Chapter 9 of the United States Code, indicating that this act is relevant to drug approval and commercial sale. The modified text allows sales before approval, which is a violation of this law.
